author: Ṛṣi Mārkaṇḍeya (Ṛgveda 7.59.12)
mantra_sanskrit:
ॐ त्र्यम्बकं यजामहे सुगन्धिं पुष्टिवर्धनम् ।
उर्वारुकमिव बन्धनान् मृत्योर्मुक्षीय माऽमृतात् ॥
mantra_transliteration:
Oṃ tryambakaṃ yajāmahe sugandhiṃ puṣṭivardhanam |
urvārukam iva bandhanān mṛtyor mukṣīya mā'mṛtāt ||

word_by_word.Oṃ: The primordial sacred syllable; the universal vibration that precedes all creation and connects the chanter to the divine source.
word_by_word.tryambakam: The Three-Eyed One (tri = three, ambaka = eye); refers to Lord Śiva whose third eye represents omniscience — the eye of wisdom that sees past, present, and future.
word_by_word.yajāmahe: We worship, we meditate upon, we honour with devotion (from the root 'yaj' meaning to worship through offering and sacrifice).
word_by_word.sugandhim: The Fragrant One; He whose divine presence permeates everywhere like a sweet fragrance; also signifies the spiritual essence that naturally emanates from righteousness.
word_by_word.puṣṭi: Nourishment, fullness, prosperity — the sustaining energy that supports all life.
word_by_word.vardhanam: The One who increases, who causes to grow and flourish; the augmenter of nourishment and well-being.
word_by_word.puṣṭivardhanam: He who nourishes and augments all living beings; the sustainer who increases health, strength, wealth, and spiritual well-being.
word_by_word.urvārukam: A ripe cucumber or melon (urvāru); used as a metaphor for the soul that, when fully ripened through spiritual maturity, naturally detaches.
word_by_word.iva: Like, just as — a particle of comparison connecting the metaphor of the ripe fruit to the soul's liberation.
word_by_word.bandhanān: From bondage, from attachment, from the stem that binds (the fruit to the vine); represents the ties of karma, fear, suffering, and ignorance that bind the soul.
word_by_word.mṛtyoḥ: From death (mṛtyu = death); not merely physical death but also the cycle of suffering, spiritual ignorance, and all forms of decay and limitation.
word_by_word.mukṣīya: May I be liberated, may I be set free (from the root 'muc' meaning to release); the heartfelt prayer for freedom from mortality and suffering.
word_by_word.mā: Not; a particle of negation — here meaning 'do not let me be separated from…'
word_by_word.amṛtāt: From immortality, from the nectar of eternal life (amṛta = deathless, the divine nectar); the state of liberation and eternal consciousness.

translation_en: Om. We worship and adore the Three-Eyed Lord Śiva, who is fragrant with divine grace and who nourishes and sustains all living beings. Just as a ripe cucumber effortlessly separates from its binding vine, may we be liberated from the grip of death and the cycle of mortality — and may we never be separated from the nectar of immortality, from the awareness of our eternal, deathless nature.
translation_hi: ॐ। हम तीन नेत्रों वाले भगवान शिव की पूजा करते हैं, जो दिव्य सुगंध से युक्त हैं और जो समस्त प्राणियों का पोषण एवं वर्धन करते हैं। जिस प्रकार एक पका हुआ ककड़ी (उर्वारुक फल) अपनी बेल के बन्धन से सहज ही अलग हो जाती है, उसी प्रकार हमें मृत्यु के बन्धन से मुक्ति मिले — और हम अमृतत्व से, अपने शाश्वत स्वरूप की चेतना से, कभी वंचित न हों।
origin: This mantra appears in the Ṛgveda, Maṇḍala 7, Sūkta 59, Verse 12 (7.59.12). It is also found in the Yajurveda (3.60) and the Atharvaveda (14.1.17). The Ṛṣi (seer) who received this mantra is Vasiṣṭha, the great sage of the seventh Maṇḍala of the Ṛgveda. However, the mantra is most famously associated with the story of young Mārkaṇḍeya, the boy-sage who was destined to die at age sixteen. According to the Śiva Purāṇa, when Yama (the lord of death) came to claim Mārkaṇḍeya, the boy clung to the Śivaliṅga and chanted this mantra with total devotion. Lord Śiva appeared, defeated Yama, and granted Mārkaṇḍeya eternal youth — making him forever sixteen. The mantra is thus also called the 'Mṛta-Sañjīvanī Mantra' (the mantra that revives the dead) and the 'Tryambaka Mantra.' It is addressed to Rudra-Śiva in His aspect as the cosmic healer and conqueror of death.
when_to_chant: Traditionally, the Mahāmṛtyuñjaya Mantra is chanted during the following occasions: (1) During serious illness — for oneself or on behalf of a loved one who is suffering from disease or undergoing surgery. (2) On birthdays and anniversaries — as a prayer for longevity and continued health. (3) During the Mahāśivarātri festival — the great night of Śiva, when this mantra's power is said to be amplified manifold. (4) At the time of death or dying — to ease the transition of the departing soul and bring peace to those present. (5) During eclipses (Grahaṇa) — considered an especially potent time for mantra siddhi. (6) During the Śrāddha ceremonies — annual rites for departed ancestors. (7) Every Monday (Somavāra) — the day sacred to Lord Śiva. (8) During the Pradoṣa Kāla — the auspicious twilight period. In modern life, this mantra is chanted daily as a spiritual shield — before a medical appointment, during anxiety episodes, when receiving difficult health news, before long travels, or simply as part of a morning sādhana (spiritual practice) to cultivate inner fearlessness and resilience.
benefits: Protection from untimely death (akāla mṛtyu) — the most ancient and primary traditional purpose of this mantra.
Healing from physical illness — the mantra is traditionally chanted 108 times over water, which is then given to the sick person to drink.
Relief from chronic fear, anxiety, and panic — the vibration calms the nervous system and anchors the mind in a sense of divine protection.
Comfort during grief and bereavement — the mantra reframes death as liberation, helping mourners find philosophical peace.
Removal of negative planetary influences — in Jyotiṣa (Vedic astrology), this mantra is prescribed as a remedy for malefic placements, especially Saturn and Rāhu.
Cultivating fearlessness (abhaya) — regular chanting dissolves deep-seated existential fear and builds spiritual courage.
Enhancing vitality and longevity — the word 'puṣṭivardhanam' directly invokes increasing nourishment of body, mind, and spirit.

Modern wellness perspective: The rhythmic chanting regulates breathing to approximately 4-6 breaths per minute, activating the parasympathetic nervous system and reducing cortisol levels, which is similar to clinically studied breathwork techniques.

Modern wellness perspective: Repetitive sacred chanting has been shown in studies to lower blood pressure, reduce heart rate variability imbalance, and produce a meditative state comparable to deep relaxation.
Spiritual purification — the mantra burns through karmic bondage and accelerates inner awakening, making it one of the most powerful mantras in the entire Vedic tradition.
Creating a protective energy field — daily chanting is believed to generate an auric shield (kavaca) around the practitioner and their family.
pronunciation_guide:
The mantra has 33 syllables divided across two pādas (lines). Below is a syllable-by-syllable breakdown with stress and duration guidance:

=== LINE 1 ===

Oṃ — Pronounced 'AUM' (not 'OM'). Three sounds merge: 'A' (from the throat), 'U' (rolling forward through the mouth), 'M' (lips close, nasal humming). Hold the 'M' vibration for 2-3 seconds. This syllable alone is a complete meditation.

tryam·ba·kaṃ — TRY-am-ba-kam. 'Try' rhymes with English 'tree' but shorter. 'Am' as in 'umbrella.' 'Ba' as in 'butter.' 'Kaṃ' — the anusvāra (ṃ) means nasalize the ending: 'kum' with a nasal ring, like 'kung' without the 'g.'

ya·jā·ma·he — ya-JAA-ma-hey. 'Ya' as in 'yoga.' 'Jā' is long — hold it twice as long as 'ja.' 'Ma' as in 'mama.' 'He' as in 'hey.' Stress falls on 'JĀ.'

su·gan·dhiṃ — su-gan-DHIM. 'Su' as in 'sugar.' 'Gan' as in 'gun.' 'Dhiṃ' — 'dh' is an aspirated 'd' (place tongue on teeth, release with a breath). The 'iṃ' is nasalized. Stress falls on 'DHIṂ.'

puṣ·ṭi·var·dha·nam — PUSH-ti-var-dha-num. 'Puṣ' — the 'ṣ' is a retroflex 'sh' (tongue curls back toward palate). 'Ṭi' — retroflex 't' (tongue strikes the roof of the mouth, not the teeth). 'Var' as in 'varna.' 'Dha' — aspirated 'd.' 'Nam' — nasal ending.

=== LINE 2 ===

ur·vā·ru·kam — ur-VAA-ru-kam. 'Ur' as in 'poor' (short). 'Vā' is long — 'vaah.' 'Ru' as in 'rude' but short. 'Kam' as in 'come.'

i·va — I-va. 'I' as in 'it.' 'Va' as in 'vow' but short. Quick, light syllables.

ban·dha·nān — ban-dha-NAAN. 'Ban' as in 'bun.' 'Dha' — aspirated 'd.' 'Nān' is long — hold it: 'naahn.' This length is important for the metre.

mṛ·tyor — MRIT-yor. 'Mṛ' — the ṛ is a vocalic 'r,' pronounced like 'mri' in 'MRI scan.' 'Tyor' — 'ty' blend as in 'tune' (British), then 'or.'

muk·ṣī·ya — muk-SHEE-ya. 'Muk' as in 'muck.' 'Ṣī' — retroflex 'sh' with long 'ee.' 'Ya' as in 'yoga.' Stress on 'ṢĪ.'

mā — MAAH. Long vowel. Hold it.

amṛ·tāt — a-mri-TAAT. 'A' as in 'about.' 'Mṛ' same as above. 'Tāt' — long 'aa' ending with a final 't' (tongue on teeth).

=== RHYTHM & METRE ===
The mantra is in the Anuṣṭubh metre (8 syllables per quarter). Chant in a steady, unhurried rhythm. Each line should take approximately 8-10 seconds. Between the two lines, take one natural breath. The overall tempo should feel like a resting heartbeat — calm, steady, life-affirming.
chanting_instructions:
=== PREPARATION ===

1. TIME: The most auspicious time is Brahma Muhūrta (approximately 4:00–5:30 AM), but the mantra can be chanted at any time of need. Other excellent times include sunset (Sandhyā Kāla), Monday mornings, and the Pradoṣa period (1.5 hours before sunset on Trayodaśī).

2. PLACE: Choose a clean, quiet space. Face East (for health) or North (for spiritual progress). If you have a home altar, sit before it. If not, any peaceful spot will do — even a quiet corner of your bedroom.

3. BATH & CLEANLINESS: Ideally, bathe or at least wash your hands, feet, and face before beginning. Wear clean, comfortable clothes — white or light-coloured is traditional.

4. ĀSANA (POSTURE): Sit cross-legged on a mat or cushion (Sukhāsana or Padmāsana). If floor-sitting is difficult, a chair is perfectly fine — keep both feet flat on the ground, spine straight. The key is a stable, comfortable posture you can maintain for 25-35 minutes without fidgeting.

5. DĪPA & DHŪPA: If possible, light a ghee lamp (dīpa) and incense (dhūpa) before Lord Śiva's image or a Śivaliṅga. This is recommended but not mandatory.

=== THE 108 REPETITIONS ===

6. MĀLĀ (ROSARY): Use a Rudrākṣa mālā of 108+1 beads — this is the most traditional and sacred for Śiva mantras. A Sphaṭika (crystal) mālā is also excellent. Hold the mālā in your right hand. Drape it over your middle finger. Use your thumb to advance one bead per repetition. Do NOT use the index finger (it represents ego/ahaṃkāra). The mālā should not touch the ground or go below your navel.

7. SAṄKALPA (INTENTION): Before beginning, close your eyes, take three deep breaths, and silently state your intention: 'I chant this Mahāmṛtyuñjaya Mantra 108 times for [healing/protection/peace/the well-being of ___]. Oṃ Namaḥ Śivāya.'

8. CHANTING PACE: Each repetition takes approximately 12-15 seconds. Therefore, 108 repetitions will take approximately 22-28 minutes. Do not rush. Let each syllable be fully formed and felt.

9. VOLUME: You may chant in three modes — (a) Vācika: aloud, so your ears hear clearly — best for beginners and for generating strong vibrations in the space. (b) Upāṃśu: whispered, with only lip movement visible — considered ten times more powerful than loud chanting. (c) Mānasika: completely silent, mental chanting — considered one hundred times more powerful, but requires deep concentration. Begin with Vācika for the first few weeks, then progress.

10. BREATHING: Inhale naturally before each repetition. Chant the entire mantra in one breath if possible. If not, breathe at the natural pause between the two lines. Never gasp or strain.

11. SUMERU BEAD: The 109th bead (the Meru or Sumeru) is larger and marks the beginning/end of the mālā. When you reach it after 108 repetitions, do NOT cross over it. If doing multiple rounds (2 or 3 mālās), flip the mālā and go back in the opposite direction.

12. MULTIPLE ROUNDS: For serious illness or crisis, chant 3 mālās (324 repetitions) or even 11 mālās (1,188 repetitions). For daily maintenance, 1 mālā (108) is sufficient. Even 11 or 27 repetitions carry great benefit if time is limited.

=== AFTER CHANTING ===

13. CLOSING: After completing your rounds, sit quietly for 2-5 minutes with eyes closed. Feel the vibration resonating in your body. Offer the merit of your chanting to Lord Śiva and to all beings who are suffering.

14. MANTRA-CHARGED WATER: A powerful practice is to keep a copper or silver vessel of clean water beside you while chanting. The water absorbs the mantra vibrations. After chanting, this water can be sipped by yourself or given to someone who is ill. This is called 'Abhimantrita Jala.'

15. CONSISTENCY: The full power of this mantra unfolds through daily, disciplined practice over 40 days (one Maṇḍala). Commit to chanting 108 times daily for 40 consecutive days without a break. This is called a Maṇḍala Sādhana and is the traditional method for mantra siddhi (mastery).

modern_applications.health: For NRI families far from home, health anxieties can be amplified — an aging parent's surgery in India, a child's sudden illness with no extended family nearby, or one's own diagnosis received in a sterile foreign hospital. The Mahāmṛtyuñjaya Mantra serves as a profound anchor during these times. Chanting it does not replace medical treatment, but it addresses what medicine cannot — the existential terror, the helplessness, the spiritual void that opens when mortality becomes real. The mantra's inherent rhythm regulates breathing and activates the body's relaxation response, lowering stress hormones that otherwise impede healing. Many practitioners report chanting 108 rounds before surgeries, during chemotherapy sessions, or in hospital waiting rooms, finding that it transforms passive dread into active, focused devotion. The word 'puṣṭivardhanam' (nourisher of life-force) is particularly relevant — it is a prayer not just for survival, but for full, robust vitality. In a modern wellness context, consider this mantra as a spiritual complement to your health regimen, a way to engage the healing power of faith, breath, and focused intention.
modern_applications.grief: When death touches our lives — the loss of a parent back in India when you could not be there in time, the devastating phone call that arrives during a work meeting, the guilt of choosing career over presence — grief for NRI families carries unique, compounding layers of distance and regret. The Mahāmṛtyuñjaya Mantra offers comfort not by denying death, but by reframing it. The imagery of the ripe cucumber (urvārukam) gently separating from its vine teaches that death, when it comes at the right time, is not a violent severing but a natural, painless release. The soul is not destroyed — it is freed. Chanting this mantra for a departed loved one is a profound act of love across the boundary of death. It is traditionally believed that the vibrations reach the departed soul and ease its journey. For the living, the repetitive, meditative quality of 108 chants creates a container for grief — a structured time and space where sorrow is honoured, not suppressed. Many find that tears flow freely during the first few rounds and gradually give way to a deep, surprising peace by the end. This mantra says: death is real, but so is the deathless nature within us all.
modern_applications.fear: Modern life generates a constant undercurrent of fear — fear of job loss in an uncertain economy, fear of visa complications that could uproot your entire family, fear of a health scare in a country without your support system, fear of not being enough as a parent, spouse, or professional. The Mahāmṛtyuñjaya Mantra directly addresses the root of all fear: the fear of death and annihilation. By confronting the ultimate fear head-on — 'mṛtyormukṣīya' (free me from death) — all lesser fears are put into perspective and gradually lose their grip. The mantra builds 'abhaya' (fearlessness) not through denial or bravado, but through deep spiritual alignment with the eternal. When you truly internalize 'mā amṛtāt' (may I never be separated from my deathless nature), everyday anxieties about performance reviews, mortgage payments, or immigration paperwork become manageable — still real, but no longer existentially threatening. The mantra becomes an internal refuge. Many practitioners report that after a 40-day discipline of daily chanting, their baseline anxiety decreases noticeably and they develop a calm resilience that colleagues and family members remark upon.
modern_applications.daily: As a daily practice, the Mahāmṛtyuñjaya Mantra functions as a spiritual shield and a centering ritual. For NRI professionals navigating the relentless pace of modern life — early morning conference calls with India, demanding Western work cultures, weekend Tamil or Hindi school runs for children, the constant code-switching between cultures — this mantra provides a non-negotiable anchor of sacred identity. Chanting 108 times each morning takes only 25 minutes but transforms the quality of the entire day. It is a declaration: before I am an employee, a taxpayer, a visa-holder, I am a child of the Divine. The protection invoked is not superstitious — it is the protection of perspective, of remembering what truly matters, of carrying an unshakeable inner stillness into the chaos of commutes, deadlines, and WhatsApp family group dramas. Families can chant together on weekends, teaching children the pronunciation and meaning, thereby transmitting not just a mantra but an entire worldview — that life is sacred, that we are connected to an ancient lineage of seekers, and that no matter how far from Bhārat we live, Śiva's grace knows no geographical boundary.
